title: Low water continuity risk with deteriorating hydraulic stability
summary: The main irrigation water tank is low and replenishment remains uncertain, while unstable flow and worsening pressure behavior reduce confidence that controlled rationing can still be executed safely.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse may be approaching a point where low reserves and deteriorating hydraulic performance make controlled irrigation mitigation unreliable, increasing the likelihood that a stricter interruption-oriented response is needed.
symptoms: The main tank level is low and creates a serious continuity constraint during active production conditions.
Incoming replenishment remains uncertain and cannot be assumed as guaranteed in the short term.
Main line flow is no longer fully stable, which reduces confidence in the ability to sustain controlled irrigation cycles reliably.
Pressure behavior is worsening, suggesting that hydraulic execution may be deteriorating in addition to the scarcity problem itself.
A controlled rationing strategy may no longer be sufficient if hydraulic instability continues during the next irrigation cycles.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is critically low and threatens near-term irrigation continuity.
Flow is no longer reliably stable, which weakens confidence in controlled rationing as a safe mitigation strategy.
Pressure deterioration suggests that hydraulic execution reliability is degrading and may soon compromise irrigation delivery.
No reliable short-term replenishment commitment is confirmed, so continuity planning must assume continued scarcity pressure.

operational_context.note: The greenhouse remains in active production and upcoming irrigation cycles are still important for crop continuity.
operational_context.impact: The service should treat this as a serious operational decision with direct short-term consequences.

operational_context.note: Unlike the controlled rationing case, hydraulic behavior is now deteriorating rather than remaining stable.
operational_context.impact: The service should test whether adjustment is still safe or whether a stricter interruption-oriented response is becoming more appropriate.

operational_context.note: No guaranteed backup water reserve is confirmed for this situation.
operational_context.impact: The service should remain conservative and avoid optimistic continuity assumptions.

agronomic_context.note: Production-stage tomato crop remains sensitive to delayed or incomplete irrigation, but unreliable hydraulic delivery may also create uneven stress across sectors.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should weigh the crop risk of interruption against the risk of attempting irrigation under worsening delivery conditions.

business_context.note: The business risk comes not only from scarcity, but also from attempting continuity with a delivery system that may no longer execute the mitigation plan reliably.
business_context.impact: A weak or late decision here may create both crop damage and operational confusion.

notes: This case is designed to test whether Agro-DO can identify when low-water continuity risk has moved beyond a still-manageable rationing scenario and into a condition where deteriorating hydraulic stability may justify a stricter response than adjust_operation.
